device_requests_code.caption: You just bought a new smart TV and opened the Netflix app. The TV can't show a browser or let you type a password with a remote, so the Netflix app sends a POST to netflix.com/device/authorize with its client_id and the scopes it needs — your profile and streaming access.
device_requests_code.why: Smart TVs have no browser and no keyboard. A dedicated device authorization endpoint lets the Netflix app kick things off with a simple POST, keeping its responsibilities minimal: request a code, display it, poll for results. That's it.
device_requests_code.risk: Imagine typing your Netflix password letter-by-letter with a TV remote while everyone in the room watches the screen. That's not just painful UX — it exposes your credentials to anyone nearby. The device code flow exists to avoid exactly this.

device_receives_code.caption: Netflix's auth server responds with four things: a device_code (long, secret, 40+ characters), a user_code (short, like "WDJB-MJHT"), a verification URL (netflix.com/activate), and a polling interval of 5 seconds. Two codes, two audiences.
device_receives_code.why: The device_code is high-entropy because it's the bearer credential your TV will use to claim the token later. The user_code is kept short because you — a real person — have to type it on your phone. Netflix designed this gap intentionally: machines handle complexity, humans handle simplicity.
device_receives_code.risk: A low-entropy device_code would let an attacker brute-force codes and intercept tokens meant for other TVs. A long user_code would cause you to mistype it, get frustrated, and call support — defeating the entire point of a frictionless setup experience.

device_shows_code.caption: Your TV now shows the classic Netflix activation screen: "Go to netflix.com/activate and enter code: WDJB-MJHT". You grab your phone to continue. This is the moment the flow splits across two devices — and that split is the whole point.
device_shows_code.why: The code uses a limited character set that avoids confusing pairs like 0/O or 1/l, with a hyphen for visual grouping. Think about what's physically happening: the code has to travel from your TV screen, through your eyes, into your phone keyboard. Every character matters.
device_shows_code.risk: If Netflix's auth server doesn't rate-limit code entry attempts, an attacker on your WiFi could brute-force the limited code space — typically just 6-8 alphanumeric characters — and authorize their own device on your account instead of your TV.

user_visits_url.caption: You open netflix.com/activate on your phone and sign in with your Netflix credentials. Email, password, maybe two-factor — the full login experience happens on your phone, not your TV. Your phone's password manager and Face ID work perfectly here.
user_visits_url.why: Authentication happens on a device you already trust, where password managers, biometrics, and MFA all work seamlessly. Your TV gets the security properties of a full browser login without needing a browser on the TV itself. Clever, right?
user_visits_url.risk: If the activation URL were HTTP instead of HTTPS, or if you landed on netf1ix.com/activate (note the '1'), an attacker could harvest both the code and your Netflix credentials. Netflix must enforce HTTPS and show clear branding so you know you're in the right place.

user_enters_code.caption: You type "WDJB-MJHT" into the code field on your phone. Netflix's server matches it to the device_code your TV is holding. Now Netflix knows exactly which TV is requesting access — yours, in your living room.
user_enters_code.why: The user_code bridges two isolated channels: your TV (which knows the device_code) and your phone (which proves your identity). Typing the code is your explicit confirmation that you intend to activate THIS specific TV, preventing session fixation across devices.
user_enters_code.risk: Without requiring code entry — say the flow just used a URL with an embedded token — an attacker could text that URL to you. You'd sign in and unknowingly activate their TV on your account. Manual code entry forces physical proximity between you and the device.

user_grants_consent.caption: Netflix shows a confirmation: "Sign in to Netflix on your Living Room TV?" You tap Allow. At this point the device_code is authorized on Netflix's server, but your TV doesn't know that yet — it's still polling patiently.
user_grants_consent.why: This confirmation is your last chance to review what you're authorizing. On the TV there's no way to show a rich consent UI, but on your phone, Netflix can show which device, which profile, and what access is being granted.
user_grants_consent.risk: Without a confirmation screen, any device displaying a legitimate activation code could trick you into authorizing it. Someone at a hotel could display a code on the lobby TV and wait for an unsuspecting guest to activate it on their Netflix account.

device_polls_pending.caption: Meanwhile, your TV has been polling POST /token every 5 seconds with the device_code. Netflix's server keeps responding "authorization_pending" — you haven't approved yet on your phone. The TV shows a spinner and waits patiently.
device_polls_pending.why: The polling interval prevents your TV from hammering Netflix's token endpoint. Poll too fast and it gets a "slow_down" response. When millions of TVs worldwide are activating simultaneously, server-controlled backoff is critical to keeping the system stable.
device_polls_pending.risk: Without a mandated polling interval, a buggy TV firmware could fire thousands of requests per second, creating a denial-of-service on Netflix's token endpoint. Generic rate limiting isn't enough — the server needs per-device_code throttling, which the interval mechanism provides.

device_polls_success.caption: You tapped Allow on your phone, so the next poll from your TV succeeds. Netflix returns an access_token and a refresh_token. The device_code is consumed — it can never be used again, even if someone intercepted it during polling.
device_polls_success.why: The device_code is strictly single-use. Once exchanged for tokens, any attempt to reuse it gets rejected. Even if someone on your network sniffed the device_code while your TV was polling, they can't redeem it again after your TV already did.
device_polls_success.risk: If the device_code could be reused, an attacker who intercepted it via network sniffing could wait for you to approve, then redeem the same code to get their own copy of the tokens. Single-use consumption closes that race-condition window.

device_authenticated.caption: Your TV is now signed in to Netflix. It shows "Who's watching?" with your profile list. You never typed a password on the TV — your phone was the secure bridge. This is exactly how Netflix, YouTube, Disney+, and every smart TV app you've ever used actually works.
device_authenticated.why: Your TV only ever possessed scoped, temporary tokens — never your Netflix password. Credentials were never exposed to the TV's potentially insecure input system. If the TV is compromised, Netflix can revoke its tokens remotely without you changing your password.
device_authenticated.risk: Before OAuth, smart TV apps stored your actual password. A compromised firmware update or a factory reset failure could leak your credentials to every service you'd logged into. Token-based access means a compromised TV can only abuse its current tokens, which expire and can be revoked.

validation.validatingSubtext: Waiting for user to tap Allow

insights.title: Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628)
insights.description: Built specifically for devices that can't open a browser or don't have a proper keyboard for input.

sections.heading: Where You've Seen This in the Wild
items: Netflix on smart TVs shows "Go to netflix.com/activate" — that's this exact flow
YouTube, Disney+, HBO Max, and Spotify all use it on TVs and game consoles
GitHub CLI uses it when you run gh auth login and get a code to enter in your browser
Azure CLI supports it with az login --use-device-code for headless environments

sections.heading: Understanding the Polling Cycle
items: Your TV polls every N seconds, with the interval dictated by Netflix's server
An "authorization_pending" response means keep polling — you haven't approved yet
A "slow_down" response means back off and add 5 seconds to the interval
An "expired_token" response means the code timed out — start over
A 200 OK means you approved and the tokens are in the response body

sections.heading: Security Properties
items: The user_code has enough entropy to prevent guessing within its short validity window
Device codes expire after a limited time, typically around 15 minutes
Rate limiting on the polling endpoint prevents brute-force attempts by misbehaving devices
You authenticate on your own trusted phone, so credentials never touch the TV
